# Page 3^page=3
General Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 3 Executive Summary MX-Port Configuration “Leo” The scope of this document is on a requirement and concept level and not on a technical implementation level. This document considers all overall requirements of the Factory-X use cases either • through a conceptual implementation, which is described in this document, • or the implementation is in the responsibility of the application or user, • or it is explicitly stated that a requirement is not implemented and out of scope. Leo is based on successive levels “Stepn”. Companies submitting to the rules underlying “Stepn+1” can also share data with companies submitting to the rules of “Stepn” only. For a company to progress from “Stepn” to “Stepn+1”, an investment is required. Regarding governance, additional rules must be met, and regarding technology, additional software services must be implemented. Leo's minimum requirement is that the interaction of business and legacy applications is implemented by an interaction of AAS servers and AAS clients. Regarding implementation, only the specifications according to IEC 63278 respectively IDTA must be considered. The main levels of the MX-Port configuration “Leo” are: • Step 1 is characterized by standardized data exchange based on MX Gate, MX Converter, and MX Adapter, because making the own data understandable and accessible is an essential prerequisite for any cross-company data exchange. Regarding MX Discovery and MS Access and Usage Control, the two partners agree individually at this level. • Step 2 is characterized by standardized MX Discovery. The basis is a list of standardized company lookup information through which a data consumer can find a data provider's AAS server. The data consumer can maintain the list entries itself or use a 3 rd party operating company. • Step 3 is characterized by standardized MS Access and Usage Control. The basis is a list of trusted partner entries. The data provider can maintain the list itself or use a 3rd party operating company. The use of a 3 rd party operating company simplifies the onboarding of new partners to the data ecosystem. Leo supports the integration of OPC-UA by making OPC-UA data available as AAS models via MX Adapters and MX Converters. MX Discovery and MX Access and Usage Control of Leo can also be used in combination with an MX Gate to MX Gate communication implemented by OPC UA (instead of being implemented via an AAS interface as in the case of Leo). Leo enables the specification and enforcement of access policies by a data provider. The AAS client accesses the data provider with a token in accordance with the IEC 63278 respectively IDTA specification. Two mechanisms are supported to ensure that this token can be accepted by the AAS server • Tokens are issued by the data provider and the effort of managing the tokens lies with the data consumer. • The data consumer manages its own tokens and registers its trusted partner entry on a list of trusted partners. The data provider verifies the token submitted by the data consumer based on the trusted partner entry of the data consumer and exchanges it to a token, which can be accepted by its AAS server.

# Page 4^page=4
General Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 4 Leo enables the specification of usage policies by a data provider by attaching them to data provided to a data consumer. Leo does not support contract negotiations regarding usage policies. Leo does not support technical enforcement of usage policies by the data consumer. Regarding operation, Leo does not require a 3rd party operating company. Leo enables a 3rd party operating company that manages a list of company lookup information or trusted partner entries. There can be several 3 rd party operating companies each managing their own list of company lookup information or trusted partner entries. Governance for the MX-Port configuration “Leo” will be further elaborated within the remaining Factory-X project timeframe Table of content 1 General ..............................................................................6 1.1 Starting position .................................................................... 6 1.2 Purpose and scope ............................................................... 6 1.3 Data Sharing in Manufacturing-X .......................................... 7 1.4 Objectives and approach of Factory-X .................................. 8 1.4.1 General ............................................................................................... 8 1.4.2 Step 1: standardized common data models and data access .............. 9 1.4.3 Step 2: standardized discovery of the data provider ............................ 9 1.4.4 Step 3: standardized concepts for data sovereignty and trust .............10 2 Overview ..........................................................................10 2.1 Asset Administration Shell ................................................... 10 2.2 Application of AAS in MX-Port configuration “Leo” .............. 11 2.3 Identification of assets and AASs ........................................ 11 2.4 Overall technical setup of MX-Port configuration “Leo” ........ 12 2.5 High-level cross-company workflow .................................... 13 3 MX Gate ...........................................................................14 3.1 Requesting information from an AAS server........................ 14 3.2 Cross-company data exchange ........................................... 14 3.3 Company internal data exchange ........................................ 14 3.4 OPC-UA as integrated technology ...................................... 15 4 MX Discovery ..................................................................15

# Page 6^page=6
General Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 6 1 General 1.1 Starting position Factory-X will develop a digital ecosystem for factory outfitters and factory operators and considers a large and diverse range of machine builders, component suppliers, factory operators and software application providers. Therefore, Factory-X is moving beyond a “one- size-fits-all” approach based on the concept of a modular MX-Port, see [1]. The modular MX- Port enables that software applications are integrated in a uniform manner and existing software applications can be integrated without having to change them. The modular MX-Port has a layered structure with different functional manifestations per layer and a use case, or a data ecosystem can configure a specific MX-Port according to its needs. For more details see [2] and [3]. In a first step, Factory-X uses the modular MX-Port to realize an MX-Port configuration “Leo” and an MX-Port configuration “Hercules”, see Figure 1 for illustration. Figure 1: Using the modular MX-Port to realize the MX-Port configurations “Leo” and “Hercules” The MX-Port configuration “Leo” is compliant with IEC 63278, see [4] / 61406, see [5] / IDTA and uses the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) as integration technology for cross-company data sharing1. 1.2 Purpose and scope The purpose of this document is to describe the MX-Port configuration “Leo”. The document describes (logical) concepts and no implementation aspects. It is feasible regarding different technical implementations and implementation technologies2. The aim of the document is to be understandable even for non-software architects. In particular, the product owner of the Factory-X use cases should be able to evaluate the contents of this document regarding the impact on their use case. This document continues the Factory-X Use Cases Application Guide, see [2], and describes a design decision to implement the technical view of the Factory-X use cases (based on MX- Port) using the AAS concept. To put it simple, business applications acting as responders are 1 The MX-Port concept is designed to be used for both intra-company and cross-company data exchange. For intra- company data exchange, “lightweight” concepts can be used for the MX Discovery and MX Access & Usage Control layers. 2 It is the task of the Factory-X architecture team to make suggestions as to the extent to which reuse of existing implementations is possible.

# Page 7^page=7
General Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 7 implemented by an AAS server, and business applications acting as requesters are implemented by an AAS client. 1.3 Data Sharing in Manufacturing-X The overarching goal of Manufacturing-X is to provide means for sharing data across companies and for generating additional value based on it. To achieve this, companies must fulfill three basic requirements: • Common data models and data access: companies that share data have a common understanding of the content of the shared data (otherwise, the exchanged data simply has no value). To achieve this, companies must agree on such a common understanding, which is typically captured in a shared information model addressing aspects of terminology and semantics. The company that provides the data must prepare the internally available data in some representation form that is in line with the shared information model to reflect the common understanding. In addition to a common understanding, it is useful to agree on uniform mechanisms for accessing the data and its semantics. • Discovery of the data provider: a company that provides data must be discoverable for companies that want to use this data. To achieve this, a company providing data must make itself known to other companies. This also includes that the company providing data must provide a mechanism for accessing this data from outside the company based on concepts provided by Manufacturing-X, for example a common infrastructure or shared services. • Data sovereignty of the data provider and mutual trust: a company that provides data wants to determine for itself to whom and under what conditions it makes this data available; moreover, the companies that share data must trust each other. This includes in the event of external access that the data provider can be sure that a data consumer is really who it claims to be. This also includes that the data consumer can be sure that the data provided truly originates from the data provider and has not been tampered. This might also include rules, policies or contracts that describe what the data consumer is allowed to do with the requested data. Today companies clarify these basic requirements among themselves in an individual manner. The innovation hypothesis of Manufacturing-X is to replace these individual clarifications with general rules3. Companies that want to share data then submit to these general rules because this simplifies their cross-company data sharing. Rules4 can include technical, organizational and contractual aspects. There must be intrinsic motivation for the individual companies to agree to these general rules. Agreeing with a set of rules results in an investment for the individual companies. This investment must be justified by the expected benefits from the set of rules. Individual companies are in different starting positions; therefore, the necessary investments differ. Individual companies have different goals; in this respect, the assessment of expected benefits from the set of rules differs. Examples of the different goals of cross-company data sharing are • Providing additional computer-processable information about a physical product. 3 In addition to the rules, accompanying technical concepts and prototype solutions are provided in the individual Manufacturing-X projects. 4 An organization that determines which rules apply is called a governance body, for details see chapter 7.

# Page 8^page=8
General Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 8 • Offering as-a-service business models (leasing machines, providing manufacturing capacities, etc.). • Compliance with regulatory requirements (EU digital product passport, EU data act, PCF-reporting, FDA or GAMP in the pharmaceutical industry, etc.). • Understanding and improving a complex manufacturing process together because one company alone is not capable of doing this due to a lack of data. 1.4 Objectives and approach of Factory-X 1.4.1 General Factory-X aims to ensure that as many factory outfitters and factory operators as possible submit to these general rules. Therefore, the general rules must be designed in such a way that the ratio of necessary investment to expected benefit is economically attractive for as many companies as possible. A balance must be struck so that the entry barrier is not set too high – otherwise there is a risk that not enough companies will submit to the general rules – but also not too low – otherwise there is a risk that these general rules do not simplify cross- company data sharing sufficiently. Due to the different starting positions and goals of the individual companies, Factory-X pursues a solution approach in the form of successive steps building on each other (roadmap for the companies). The individual companies can initially focus on the step whose implementation offers them the most suitable cost/benefit ratio regarding their individual starting position and goal. After implementing the selected step, companies have the option of implementing further steps based on this to achieve further benefits through additional investments. Figure 2 illustrates successive steps building on each other of Factory-X. Note that the steps are modular in the sense that step 2 and step 3 can be swapped. A key requirement emerging from the Factory-X use cases is that cross-company data sharing is possible if a data provider provides an AAS server or a data consumer provides an AAS client in accordance with the specifications of IEC 63278 / IEC 61406 / IDTA. Figure 2: MX-Port configuration “Leo” paves the way for companies to be able to share data with many partners in the future (illustration) • Step 1: standardized common data models and data access: The necessary requirements regarding the discovery of data provider, the data sovereignty of the data provider, and mutual trust of data provider and data consumer will be clarified among the affected companies individually, as is the case today. • Step 2: standardized discovery of the data provider: The necessary requirements regarding the data sovereignty of the data provider, and mutual trust of data provider

# Page 9^page=9
General Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 9 and data consumer will be clarified among the affected companies individually, as is the case today. • Step 3: standardized concepts for data sovereignty and trust: The basic concept is a list of trusted partners, which can be maintained by the data provider or a 3rd party operating company. The list of trusted partners can be implemented in various forms and there are various options the list can be maintained. It still needs to be analyzed in more detail which of these options will be implemented prototypically in the Factory-X project. The MX-Port configuration “Leo” addresses the implementation of all steps 1 to 3. 1.4.2 Step 1: standardized common data models and data access Initial Situation: Manufacturing companies are increasingly feeling the pressure to make data available for various purposes and partners (suppliers, customers, authorities, etc.) in a computer-processable format. However, many manufacturing companies (>90%) are currently unable to make their diverse internal data available in a way that others can easily understand and process. Often, the data that they initially want to share with other partners is public or available only in a proprietary format. Why: Step 1 creates the conditions for manufacturing companies to make the necessary, but sometimes significant, investments so that they can gradually implement the increasing requirements. What: Manufacturing companies gradually convert internal data into a uniform representation that supports an explicit encoding of semantics utilizing standardized data models, so that the data becomes understandable in terms of content. Furthermore, they implement standardized interfaces for providing access to data according to these data models, so that the data becomes easily accessible through well-defined access paths at a suitable granularity. How: Focus on MX Gate, MX Converter, and MX Adapter. Adapters play the role of mediating between proprietary forms of access to legacy systems and the desired harmonized access methods. Converters transform payloads of adapted messages to the representation form that adheres to the standardized common data models, both syntactically and semantically. Gates expose the thus converted information to the outside communication partner that conforms with the MX-Port ensuring standardized common data access. 1.4.3 Step 2: standardized discovery of the data provider Initial Situation: The manufacturing companies providing data want to determine for themselves whom and under what conditions they make their data available. They have established solutions in place to secure their own data and want to use these existing solutions to share data with companies and partners with whom they already have established business relationships. They are developing initial business cases for cross-company data sharing and are testing market entry with selected pilot customers and partners; they therefore initially have no need to share data with many companies and partners. However, companies are increasingly faced with the burden of administering their established solutions. Therefore, they want to be able to organize discovery and administration information consistently across companies. Why: Step 2 allows the data providers to make themselves and their capabilities known to data consumers by registering themself and their capabilities in a standardized way. Step 2 creates the opportunity for manufacturing companies to share sensitive data with other companies based on existing solutions while maintaining their data sovereignty, but to reduce the administrative effort of established solutions.

# Page 10^page=10
Overview Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 10 What: Manufacturing companies that want to provide data use the solutions they use to secure their own data and negotiate interactions with other companies and partners bilaterally and they offer external software applications standardized access to their own data or software applications. In addition, companies reduce the administrative effort of their established solutions through standardized discovery and administration information across companies. Especially when sharing data with existing partners, they can be informed about configuration changes in a uniform and automated manner and can also easily integrate new partners from a technical perspective. A standardized discovery can be deployed centrally and at any data consumer allowing the data provider to register itself and its capabilities. The data provider has full control what information is shared and who can access it, what is available centrally, what locally with restricted access at the data provider and what is not shared. Step 2 enables mixed operation with Step 1 in the sense that companies submitting to the rules underlying Step 2 can also share data with companies based on the rules of Step 1. How: Focus on MX Discovery and company-specific implementation of FX Access and Usage Control. 1.4.4 Step 3: standardized concepts for data sovereignty and trust Initial Situation: Manufacturing companies are increasingly sharing their data with more companies and partners and are recognizing the increasing complexity of their specific solutions because bilaterally negotiated interactions and individually defined access and usage policies are reaching their limits in terms of distribution and scalability. They are increasingly understanding the advantages and benefits of 3rd party operating companies for identification and authentication, but do not want to be locked in by a specific 3rd party operating company. Companies are also increasingly understanding the advantages and benefits of a tightly knit consortium regarding onboarding new companies and partners. Why: Step 3 creates the opportunity for manufacturing companies to extend their specific solutions based on bilaterally negotiated interactions to scale to many companies and partners. What: Manufacturing companies use a list of trusted partners and integrate their existing identity and access management systems into this concept. This allows identification and authentication to be standardized across companies while still allowing companies to continue using their existing identity and access management systems. In addition to the advantages of this technical standardization, this list of trusted partners can also be maintained by a 3rd party operating company of their own choice. In this case, the onboarding of new business partners can also be managed centrally by the 3rd party operating company and companies must answer the question of what level of trust they want to achieve and which procedural issues they are willing to leave to such a 3rd party operating company. Step 3 enables mixed operation with Step 2 in the sense that companies submitting to the rules underlying Step 3 can also share data with companies based on the rules of Step 2 or Step 1. How: Focus on MX Access and Usage Control with onboarding, identification and authentication by one or multiple 3 rd party operating companies. 2 Overview 2.1 Asset Administration Shell This document considers the concept of AAS, and not its implementation. The basis for these considerations is the internationally agreed standard IEC 63278-1, see [4]. Figure 3 shows the overview of the AAS and related entities as considered in this document. This document supplements the terminology of IEC 63278-1 with the concept of an AAS server. An AAS server is a software service that manages a set of AASs, understood as a concept, and not as an

# Page 11^page=11
Overview Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 11 implementation. For reasons of symmetry, the term AAS client is also used, but this is a synonym for AAS user application. Figure 3: Detailed overview of Asset Administration Shell and related entities, see [4] 2.2 Application of AAS in MX-Port configuration “Leo” This chapter describes how the concepts of business applications, legacy applications, MX- Port as described in [2] are conceptually mapped to the AAS concepts shown in Figure 3. If business or legacy applications want to exchange data, according to [2], one business or legacy application acts as requester and the other one as responder. The MX-Port configuration “Leo” is characterized by the following design decision: • A business or legacy application acting as requester shall be implemented by an AAS client wrapping the business or legacy application. This means that the AAS client requests data by accessing the AAS interfaces of the AASs managed by an AAS server. • A business or legacy application acting as responder shall be implemented by an AAS server connected to the business logic of the business or legacy application and mapping to its internal data model. This means that the AAS server provides the requested data in the form of AASs/Submodels/SubmodelElements and, for this purpose, provides the appropriate AAS interfaces for the managed AASs. It is now the responsibility of the individual Factory-X use cases to make the following decisions: • What are the assets considered in the use case? • What are the AASs associated to the assets including the Submodels and Submodel- Elements of the AASs in the use case? • Which AAS user applications will be provided? • Which asset related services, asset integration and asset services will be provided? • Which Submodel templates and concept repositories are used? The requirements defined by the governance body, see chapter 7, must also be considered. 2.3 Identification of assets and AASs By choosing AAS as the integration technology for the MX-Port configuration “Leo”, exchanged data always relates to an asset. Each asset has an asset ID as a unique identifier in accordance with IEC 61406, which in the case of physical assets can be applied as a label to the asset. The asset ID does not change when ownership of the asset is transferred. This is

# Page 12^page=12
Overview Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 12 illustrated in Figure 4 by the fact that the component that the machine builder integrates into its machine has the same label as the component supplied by the component supplier, and the machine supplied by the machine builder has the same label as the machine used by the factory operator. With their component or machine, the component supplier or machine builder will deliver selected information about their component or machine, which the machine builder or factory operator can integrate into their own AASs. This is illustrated in Figure 4 by the fact that there can be multiple AASs for an asset, which are governed by different AAS responsibles and are therefore colored differently. These AASs therefore have different AAS IDs. Information about a component or machine is delivered once. If information needs to be synchronized after delivery, the application is responsible for this. IEC 63278-4 describes various ways to accomplish this. Figure 4: Illustration of asset IDs and AAS IDs Note: Whether the machine supplier discloses the installed components to the factory operator depends on the business strategy of the machine supplier 2.4 Overall technical setup of MX-Port configuration “Leo” Figure 5 provides an overview of the technical entities considered within the MX-Port configuration “Leo”. The technical entities are structured into the level’s business/legacy applications and shared services according to the “Foundational Framework for Manufacturing-X”. Figure 5: Overview of core technical entities of MX-Port configuration “Leo” Figure 6 shows the overall technical setup for the MX-Port configuration “Leo”. On the left side the technical view according to [2] is shown. On the right-hand side the logical implementation of the MX-Port configuration “Leo” based on the concept of AAS is illustrated. It shows abstract software services and illustrates their association to the logical elements on the left-hand side.

# Page 13^page=13
Overview Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 13 Figure 6: Overall technical setup of MX-Port configuration “Leo” The logical implementation of MX Gate is detailed in chapter 3; the logical implementation of MX Discovery is detailed in chapter 4; the logical implementation and operation of MX Access and Usage Control is detailed in chapter 5. Note that the MX-Port configuration “Leo” does not address the logical implementation of MX Converter and MX Adapter. 2.5 High-level cross-company workflow For cross-company data exchange, the MX-Port configuration “Leo” is based on a high-level workflow that is divided into two basic steps. Note that the MX-Port configuration “Leo” also supports access to public data without any access control. In this case MX Access and Usage Control is not required. Initial setup: before a requester can access a responder, the following activities are necessary: • There is a legal contract between the data provider and data consumer. This is an activity outside the scope of this document. • The responder provides its company lookup information. This activity is logically associated with MX Discovery and is further detailed in chapter 4. • The responder assigns access and usage rights for its data. This optional activity is logically associated with MX Access and Usage Control and is further detailed in 5.4. • The requester manages credentials, that the responder can technically verify. There are various ways in which these credentials can be issued. This optional activity is logically associated with MX Access and Usage Control and is further detailed in 5.2. • The requester manages the public key of the responder so that the responder can sign the provided data, and the requester can validate the signature. This optional activity is logically associated with MX Access and Usage Control and is further detailed in 5.6. Data request: concrete access to asset data provided by a responder from a requester follows the following steps:

# Page 14^page=14
MX Gate Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 14 • The requester uses a company lookup service to find the information on where to find the software services of the responder and excludes spoofing. This activity is logically associated with MX Discovery and is further detailed in chapter 4. • The requester provides a token to the responder, which the responder can verify. There are various ways how the requester receives the token. The requester uses the token for requesting access to the required data. There are various ways of verification by the responder. This optional activity is logically associated with MX Access and Usage Control and is further detailed in 5.3. • The responder checks whether the requester has access rights. This optional activity is logically associated with MX Access and Usage Control and is further detailed in 5.4. • The responder signs the data to be provided. This optional activity is logically associated to MX Access and Usage Control and is further detailed in 5.6. • The responder provides the data to the requester, which is addressed by MX Gate. Additionally, this activity is logically associated with MX Access and Usage Control and is further detailed in 5.4. Optionally, the responder can also link usage rights to the provided data, see 5.5. 3 MX Gate 3.1 Requesting information from an AAS server If an AAS client has access to an AAS server and requests specific information hosted by the AAS server, the MX-Port configuration “Leo” supports the following possibilities: • The AAS client knows the asset ID and requests an AAS or Submodel/ SubmodelElements of an AAS. • The AAS client specifies criteria, and requests all AASs or Submodel/ SubmodelElements of AASs meeting these criteria. Requesting information from an AAS server based on asset IDs or AAS IDs is addressed by [7] and IEC 63278-5. Requesting information from an AAS server based on specified criteria expressed in a dedicated query language is currently in discussion at IDTA, see [9]. Application example • The data consumer recognizes the discovery interface of an AAS server of a data provider based on a previously defined configuration and requests the required data from the data provider. This can be, for example, engineering tools that exchange engineering data. 3.2 Cross-company data exchange Figure 6 describes a cross-company data exchange between business/legacy applications. The AAS client accesses the AASs of the AAS server via the AAS interface, which is passed through the two MX Gates. Note that these two MX Gates are logical objects only. The access shown in Figure 6 is from the AAS client directly to the AAS interface of the corresponding AAS. 3.3 Company internal data exchange Figure 7 describes based on Figure 6 a company internal data exchange between business/legacy applications, typically provided by different software suppliers. The AAS client

# Page 15^page=15
MX Discovery Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 15 accesses the AASs of the AAS server via the AAS interface, which is passed through the MX Gate5. Figure 7: Company internal data exchange between business applications6 3.4 OPC-UA as integrated technology7 In the case that the business/legacy application 1 in Figure 7 is an OPC-UA client and the business/legacy application 2 in Figure 7 is an OPC-UA server, Figure 7 illustrates a special case of company internal data exchange, where OPC-UA is used as an integrated technology. 4 MX Discovery 4.1 General MX Discovery has the following tasks in the context of the MX-Port configuration “Leo”: • An AAS client requests information on where to find the AAS server of a known data provider, see 4.3. • An AAS client requests information about all companies that provide a specific capability. Examples of capabilities include providing a production technology or offering a product with certain properties, see 4.4. These tasks are not within the current scope of AAS and must therefore be further developed and implemented prototypically within Factory-X.8 The MX-Port configuration “Leo” allows to restrict the access to an AAS server. The technical concepts for this are described in 5.2, 5.3 and 5.4. 4.2 Company lookup information Every company acting as a data provider must define its specific company lookup information: 5 Two MX-Ports can also be used for company internal data exchange, like shown in Figure 6. For cross-company data exchange, two MX-Ports are used for governance reasons. 6 For a company internal data exchange a discovery concept for business applications and sometimes also access and usage control concepts are needed, but “lightweight” concepts for the MX Discovery and MX Access & Usage Control layers are often sufficient. 7 OPC-UA as integration technology is not in the scope of this document. This will be discussed in the context of the MX-Port configuration “Orion”. 8 Note that discovery of AASs and their Submodels/SubmodelElements is part of MX Gate, see 3.1, and not MX Discovery.

# Page 16^page=16
MX Discovery Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 16 • A company shall specify its company information, on the basis of which a company lookup service provides the company lookup information. • A company can specify which capabilities it offers to other companies. How these capabilities shall be specified must be further elaborated. • A company shall specify how another company can access the company's software services. The conventions the company information shall follow, how capabilities shall be specified and how access can be specified must be further elaborated9. 4.3 Request for finding the AAS server of a known data provider For this task there are company lookup operators each operating a company lookup service providing the necessary company lookup information when a data provider is specified by its company information. As mentioned in 5.2, a data consumer may operate its own company lookup service. The data provider shall make the discovery information of the AAS server operated on its behalf available to all company lookup operators that are of its interest. Figure 8 illustrates the conceptual approach of requesting for information on where to find the AAS server of a known data provider in the context of the MX-Port configuration “Leo”. In this case, the company lookup information managed by the company lookup service does not need to specify any capabilities that the company offers to other companies. Figure 8: Illustration of requesting information on where to find the AAS server of a known data provider Application example • The data provider delivers a product and equips it with machine-readable company lookup information, for example, a QR code according to IEC 61406, i.e. www.dummy.com/discovery?asset_id=12345, for the AAS server operated on its behalf. In this case, the company lookup service is a software service that makes the delivered machine-readable company lookup information available, for example, initiated by a human scanning the QR code. The provision of the company lookup information is part of the delivery of the product. In this case, no data provider is provided for the “requests” of the AAS client shown in Figure 8. 9 There is need for a standard specifying the company lookup information for the MX-Port configuration “Leo”. An example on implementation level is ID-Link (IEC 61406), nevertheless, this document describes the concept only.

# Page 17^page=17
MX Access and Usage Control Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 17 4.4 Request for finding companies providing a specific capability The internal setup is analogous to the request for information on where to find the AAS server of a known data provider, however, the capabilities provided must now also be specified in the company lookup information of the individual companies. In a data request, it is not the data provider that is specified, but the capability that the data provider provides, so that the company lookup service can provide all AAS servers of companies providing the requested capability, see Figure 9. Figure 9: Illustration of requesting for all companies providing a specific capability Application example • “Broadcast search” according to Requirement #14 can be implemented based on this approach. All AAS servers meeting the specific criteria register themselves at the company lookup service. 5 MX Access and Usage Control 5.1 General This document focuses on “service to service” communication between data provider and data consumer. “Service on behalf of a human to service” communication is only illustrated by an example, “human to service” communication is not addressed in this document. In addition to the case where there is no MX Access and Usage Control in the case of publicly available information, the MX-Port configuration “Leo” considers two different expansions stages: • In the first expansion stage, the data consumer receives the credentials directly from the data provider. This expansion stage addresses step 1 according to Figure 2. In this expansion stage, neither a gateway service nor a token exchange service is required. The advantage of this expansion stage is that no changes are necessary on the data provider side, thus enabling a quick and easy introduction to cross-company data sharing. However, as the number of relationships between data providers and data consumers who want to share data increases, the effort required for onboarding new partners and for managing the credentials provided by the data provider becomes increasingly significant. • In the second expansion stage, the data provider uses a list of trusted partners. This expansion stage addresses step 3 according to Figure 2. The data consumer must be added to the trusted partner list and provide suitable credentials to this list. Both the data provider and the data consumer can use their own credentials. The advantage of this expansion stage is that the trusted partner list conceptually separates the onboarding of new partners from the operational exchange of tokens. This allows

# Page 18^page=18
MX Access and Usage Control Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 18 onboarding to be managed centrally by a 3 rd party trusted partner list operator without requiring any changes to credential management by data consumer and data provider. The list of trusted partner can be implemented in various forms and there are various options which of the required shared services according to Figure 5 are operated by the data provider and data consumer and which shared services are operated by a 3rd party shared service operator. It still needs to be analyzed in more detail which of these options will be implemented prototypically in the Factory-X project. In this respect, the descriptions of the second expansion stage in 5.2 and 5.3 are merely to be seen as examples, where the data provider uses a gateway service and a token exchange service to verify the credentials provided by the data consumer. 5.2 Management of credentials The purpose of credential management is to enable the data consumer to provide credentials for authentication to the data provider, see 5.3, which the data provider can verify. Figure 10 illustrates credential management in the first expansion stage. Figure 10: Management of credentials in the first expansion stage Figure 11 illustrates as an example credential management in the second expansion stage. Figure 11: Management of credentials in the second expansion stage (example) 5.3 Authentication The purpose of the authentication is to enable the data consumer to receive a token that the data provider can verify. Figure 12 illustrates authentication in the first expansion stage.

# Page 19^page=19
MX Access and Usage Control Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 19 Figure 12: Authentication in the first expansion stage (example) Figure 13 illustrates as an example authentication for a “service to service” communication in the second expansion stage. Figure 13: Authentication for “service to service” in the second expansion stage Figure 14 illustrates as an example authentication for a “service on behalf of a human to service” communication in the second expansion stage. Figure 14: Authentication for “service on behalf of a human to service” in the second expansion stage (example) 5.4 Authorization According to 3.2, authorization requires an initial setup in which a data provider stores the access policies for the data provided by an AAS server. Access policies are specified according to IEC 63278-3 and [8].

# Page 20^page=20
MX Access and Usage Control Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 20 For a specific data request, the data consumer provides a token, see 5.3, in which a set of assertions is stored. The identity and access management service of the data provider verifies the token. If the verification is successful, the AAS server then checks whether access to the data should be granted based on the assertions stored in the token. If access should be granted, the data is transmitted to the data consumer. Figure 15 illustrates the authorization. Figure 15: Authorization 5.5 Usage control The MX-Port configuration “Leo” supports the description of usage policies and the association of usage policies when providing data. For this purpose, according to 3.2 in an initial setup the data provider stores the usage policies for the data provided by an AAS server. Usage policies are specified according to IEC 63278-3 and [8]. It is still in discussion whether the provision of usage policies will be implemented prototypically in the Factory-X project. Conceptually the MX-Port configuration “Leo” offers monitoring and reporting of provided data. As a result, a data consumer runs a high risk of being sued if it does not adhere to the usage rights formulated in the contract. The MX-Port configuration “Leo” does not support usage control in the sense that after data has been made available, it can be technically enforced that the data is only used under compliance with certain terms of use. Examples of such terms of use are prohibiting the copying of provided data or the transfer of provided data to other legal entities. 5.6 Data accountability As mentioned in 2.4, the MX-Port configuration “Leo” assumes that data provider and data consumer operate a public key infrastructure, meaning that data provider and data consumer each manage a public and a private key in their identity and access management service. On this basis, a data provider can sign provided data so that the data consumer can be sure that the data has not been tampered with. Figure 16 illustrates the signing of data; it should be noted that the workflow depicted in Figure 15 is intertwined with the workflow shown in Figure 16.

# Page 21^page=21
Operation Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 21 Figure 16: Data accountability 6 Operation Figure 17 shows the overall business setup of the MX-Port configuration “Leo”. Figure 17 is based on Figure 6, but for reasons of clarity, the logical elements of the MX-Port and its layers are not shown. The MX-Port configuration “Leo” is intended for the case that the data provider and data consumer already operate, or have operated, an IT infrastructure that includes a company-wide credential store service and identity and access management service. This includes a public key infrastructure. A gateway service is considered as part of the AAS server and a token exchange service is considered as part of the identity and access management service. They are logically separated to illustrate the overall flows. Note that the credential store service, identity and access management service, gateway service and token exchange service are not intended to be developed and implemented by Factory-X “Kernel & Basis Servcies”. The credential store services and identity and access management services existing in the companies must be extended according to the guidance of the MX-Port configuration “Leo”.

# Page 22^page=22
Governance aspects Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 22 Figure 17: Overall business setup For the MX-Port configuration “Leo”, the various business roles can be assumed by legal entities as follows: • The business role AAS client operator may be assumed by the legal entity assuming the role data consumer. • The business role AAS server operator may be assumed by the legal entity assuming the role data provider. • The business role company lookup operator may be assumed by the legal entity assuming the role AAS client operator. • The business role trusted partner list operator may be assumed by the legal entity assuming the role AAS server operator. In this case, the AAS server operator verifies the trustworthiness of another company. The business role of trusted partner list operator may also be assumed by a 3 rd party operator of shared service. In this case, all companies listed on the trusted partner list trust that the 3rd party operator has verified the trustworthiness of the companies on this list. Thus, in the extreme case, only two legal entities are involved in cross-company data exchange: one assuming the role data provider and the other the role data consumer. 7 Governance aspects 7.1 Overview Figure 18, which is an extension of Figure 27 of [2], shows the business roles that should be considered when discussing governance aspects of the MX-Port configuration “Leo” of Factory-X.

# Page 23^page=23
Governance aspects Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 23 Figure 18: Overview of governance aspects 7.2 Governance body The governance body defines the rules that all business roles must follow to participate in the MX-Port configuration “Leo” of Factory-X. This concerns the • characteristics of technical entities, such as their compliance with a certain standard, • processes to be followed regarding the development and application of technical entities, which also includes certification processes, • organizational constraints, such as which legal entity has which authority. Governance for the MX-Port configuration “Leo” will be further elaborated within the remaining Factory-X project time., For example, the following aspects will be detailed: • Detailing the concept of the MX-Port configuration “Leo” towards a technical specification • Describing the processes to be adhered to during development and use of technical entities. This includes for example o Which guidelines must a user adhere to when modeling assets? Examples include determining who is allowed to define submodel templates, which submodel templates in which version are to be used in which use case, or how general concepts such as “product type”, “product instance”, or “role objects” are to be applied. o Which guidelines must be adhered to when providing business applications or shared services, and which criteria does a certifier use to verify compliance with these guidelines? o Which guidelines must be adhered to when operating shared services? Examples include the criteria that are reviewed for initial onboarding on a list of trusted partners or the criteria that are continuously reviewed for a partner on a list of trusted partners.

# Page 24^page=24
Governance aspects Oct 08, 25 Factory-X Use Cases 24 • Enhancing the concept of the MX-Port configuration “Leo”, for example, if additional requirements shall be supported by the MX-Port configuration “Leo”. • Maintaining the overall content, for example, if the asset administration shell or the MX- Port concept evolves over time. 7.3 Standardization body/community From a conceptual perspective, the asset administration shell is the basic standard for the MX- Port configuration “Leo”. This standard is being developed and maintained by the consortium IDTA and consensus based by IEC 63278. It still needs to be defined which organization will standardize the two shared services, namely the company lookup service and the management of a trusted partner list. This could be achieved by expanding the scope of the IDTA and IEC 63278. From a conceptual perspective, additional standardization bodies are not necessary for the MX-Port configuration “Leo”. Note that as the MX-Port configuration “Leo” is detailed through a technical specification, further standards will be relevant, for example, IEC 61406 “ID-Link”. 7.4 Certifier From a technical perspective, this involves verifying compliance with IDTA specifications. IDTA is currently elaborating certification against IDTA specifications. From the perspective of the MX-Port configuration “Leo”, it is currently not necessary to initiate further certification activities from a technical perspective. Certification from a procedural perspective for the MX-Port configuration “Leo” must be elaborated, after having defined the processes and organizations regarding the governance of the MX-Port configuration “Leo”. 7.5 Provider and operator of shared service The MX-Port configuration “Leo” is based on two shared services: • company lookup service • trusted partner list management service The specific content of these lists, i.e., company lookup information and trusted partner entry, and the related services will be defined by detailing this document by a technical specification. It remains to be discussed whether and in what form an open-source implementation should be provided for them. The company lookup service can be operated by the data consumer or by one or more 3rd party operators of shared service. The trusted partner list can be managed by the data provider or one or more 3 rd party operators of shared service. Possible candidates for a 3rd party operator for these two shared services are an association (for example ZVEI or VDMA), a standardization body/community (for example IDTA with an extended business mandate) or an operating company for the MX-Port configuration “Hercules” (for example Cofinity-X). 7.6 Supporting services Supporting services for the MX port configuration “Leo” are optional. Nevertheless, for an accurate, quick and easy implementation of the MX-Port configuration “Leo”, especially at SMEs, and a fast scaling of the approach in the data ecosystem, supporting services are
